coredata.dc:title: Boron–nitrogen-doped carbon dots on multi-walled carbon nanotubes for efficient electrocatalysis of oxygen reduction reactions
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Pei, Yanfei
Song, Haoqiang
Liu, Yuan
Cheng, Yaojia
Li, Weidong
Chen, Yumei
Fan, Yanping
Liu, Baozhong
Lu, Siyu
coredata.dc:description: Cost-effective production of metal-free catalysts for the oxygen-reduction reaction (ORR), to supersede Pt-based catalysts, is challenging. Here, a three-dimensional nanocatalyst was prepared by compounding multi-wall carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) with easily modified and doped carbon dots (CDs) as sources of B and N. The catalyst has high conductivity and a large specific surface area similar to the MWCNTs, allowing exposure of many CDs with rich edge active sites and enhancing electron transfer. The catalyst exhibits excellent ORR performance, with 0.92 V of Eonset vs reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE). The E1/2 value exhibits a reduction of 50 mV compared with that of Pt/C (0.85 V) with a limited current density of 5.95 mA cm−2. The enhanced catalytic performance is attributed to the synergy of pyridine N and BC3. This work describes a simple and economical strategy for metal-free catalysts, and promotes the development of such catalysts for metal-air batteries and fuel cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
B, N co-doping
Oxygen reduction reaction
Electrocatalysis

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structural characterization and optical properties of BN-CDs. (a) XRD patterns; (b) fluorescence spectra of emission and excitation (the inset is a photograph of CDs solution under visible (left) and UV (right) light); (c) TEM image; (d-f) XPS spectra of BN-CDs; (d) C 1s, (e) N 1s, (f) B 1s.

(a) XRD patterns of BN-CDs@CNT, N-CNT, B-CNT and CNT; (b) Raman spectra; (c) TEM image of BN-CDs@CNT; (d) HRTEM image of BN-CDs@CNT; (e-i) TEM image of BN-CDs@CNT and corresponding EDX mapping images of B, C, N, and O.
XPS spectra of (a) BN-CDs@CNT; (b) C 1s; (c) N 1s; and (d) B 1s. (e) N2 adsorption–desorption isotherm plot; (f) pore-size distribution of BN-CDs@CNT.

ORR performance with O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH solution studied at a scan rate of 5 mV s−1. (a) ORR polarization curves for CNT, B-CNT, N-CNT, BN-CDs@CNT, and Pt/C (20 wt%) and (b) corresponding Eonset and E1/2 values; (c) electron-transfer number (black) and corresponding peroxide yield (blue) for BN-CDs@CNT; (d) Tafel plots derived from scheme (a); (e) chronoamperometric responses of BN-CDs@CNT and Pt/C before and after addition of 1 M methanol at 0.7 V (vs RHE); (f) long-term stability evaluation of BN-CDs@CNT and Pt/C catalysts at 0.7 V (vs RHE). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Schematic illustration of the synthesis of the BN-CDs@CNT.

head.ce:title: Boron–nitrogen-doped carbon dots on multi-walled carbon nanotubes for efficient electrocatalysis of oxygen reduction reactions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Henan Institute of Advanced Technology, Zhengzhou University, No. 100 Science Avenue, Zhengzhou City 450001, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Henan Institute of Advanced Technology, Zhengzhou University, No. 100 Science Avenue, Zhengzhou City 450001, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A multi-walled carbon nanotubes supported carbon dots co-doped with boron and nitrogen as high-efficiency oxygen reduction catalysts was synthesized through hydrothermal reaction and calcination. Pyridine N and BC3 act with synergy to significantly enhance catalytic activity. The B atom of BCO2 can be appropriately combined with O intermediates such as OH or H2O to promote adsorption on the catalyst.

Cost-effective production of metal-free catalysts for the oxygen-reduction reaction (ORR), to supersede Pt-based catalysts, is challenging. Here, a three-dimensional nanocatalyst was prepared by compounding multi-wall carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) with easily modified and doped carbon dots (CDs) as sources of B and N. The catalyst has high conductivity and a large specific surface area similar to the MWCNTs, allowing exposure of many CDs with rich edge active sites and enhancing electron transfer. The catalyst exhibits excellent ORR performance, with 0.92 V of Eonset vs reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE). The E1/2 value exhibits a reduction of 50 mV compared with that of Pt/C (0.85 V) with a limited current density of 5.95 mA cm−2. The enhanced catalytic performance is attributed to the synergy of pyridine N and BC3. This work describes a simple and economical strategy for metal-free catalysts, and promotes the development of such catalysts for metal-air batteries and fuel cells.

ce:section.ce:para: Fossil fuels are the main energy source worldwide, but their use is associated with issues such as increasing atmospheric pollution and global warming [1–4]. The development of cleaner energy technologies such as fuel cells [5] and metal-air batteries [6] is therefore gaining importance. The oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) is an essential electrochemical reaction in metal-air batteries and fuel cells, but its sluggish kinetics and high potential limits these devices’ practical application. Noble metals such as Pt currently dominate the market because of their superior performance, but high costs and scarce reserves hinder their large-scale application [7]. Further problems include their poor stability and low poisoning resistance. It is therefore a significant goal to find catalysts without noble metals (or even without any metals) but with strong activity, high stability, and high resistance to poisoning [8–13].

Heteroatom-doped composite carbon materials have attracted attention because of their low cost, simple synthesis, and excellent electrical conductivity. Low-electronegativity B and high-electronegativity N have been used to regulate the electronic structure of C to provide more active sites, with B and N acting with synergy to enhances catalytic activity [14–15]. As an example, a two-step synthesis has been reported [16] for a metal-free catalyst: first N was doped into graphene; subsequent calcination with boric acid in argon gave different B and N configurations and concentrations that were tunable by varying the calcination time. Experiments and density-functional theory (DFT) studies have shown that graphene N and BC3 play dominant roles as active centres during catalysis for the ORR, with their synergy markedly promoting the adsorption and reduction of O2. Fei [17] synthesized graphene quantum dots and graphene hybrid nanoplatelets (GQD/G) by mixing a precursor with boric acid and calcining in an NH3 atmosphere; the catalytic properties BN-doped graphene quantum dots and graphene hybrid nanoplatelets (BN-GQD/G) were similar to those of commercial Pt/C in alkaline media. The catalytic performance was attributed to the advantages of graphene, such as high electrical conductivity, large specific surface area, and carbon dots (CDs) with rich edge active sites. However, the manufacturing process was complex and hazardous. CDs have inspired extensive study due to their low cost, excellent optoelectronic performance, and their flexibility of surface modification [18–22]. As carriers, the abundant functional groups on CDs surfaces combine with metal d orbitals to form stable complexes, which have become a research focus [23,24]. However, heteroatom-doped CDs can adsorb and reduce oxygen intermediates and have good catalytic activity in terms of the ORR [25,26].

We have developed novel and low-cost B, N co-doped carbon dots (BN-CDs) loaded onto multi-wall carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs), ‘BN-CDs@CNT’. The larger specific surface area and higher degree of graphitization of the MWCNTs, compared with CDs, are conducive to exposure of catalytically active sites and effective charge transfer. Furthermore, BN-CDs can be well anchored on MWCNTs after hydrothermal reaction and pyrolysis, with the MWCNTs being doped with N and B atoms to form new active sites. Test results indicate that the BN-CDs@CNT catalyst may markedly improve kinetic properties of the ORR reaction in an alkaline medium.

The BN-CDs were synthesized by the classical hydrothermal method: 24 mmol L-aspartic acid and 24 mmol orthoboric acid were added to 180 mL deionized water, which was transferred to a 200 mL Teflon autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 8 h. The CDs solution was then concentrated by evaporation and freeze-dried to obtain solid BN-CDs. In repeat syntheses, the amount of L-aspartic acid was changed to 12 mmol or 36 mmol to prepare samples with different B/N ratios, B1N0.5-CDs and B1N1.5-CDs, respectively.

For the synthesis of BN-CDs@CNT, 400 mg BN-CDs and 150 mg MWCNTs were dispersed in 40 mL deionized water, stirred for 20 min before ultrasonication for 10 min. The solution was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 12 h. The precipitate was then centrifuged and washed three times before drying at 60 °C for 48 h in a vacuum drying oven. The obtained BN-CDs@CNT precursor was calcined at 800 °C for 4 h in an Ar atmosphere with temperature increasing at 5 °C min−1. For comparison, the 400 mg BN-CDs were replaced with 5 mmol L-aspartic acid, 5 mmol orthoboric acid, or with no BN-CDs, named N-CNT, B-CNT, and CNT, respectively. The resulting compounds were calcined at 800 °C for 4 h.

The preparation process of BN-CDs@CNT is shown in Scheme 1 . First, the BN-CDs was synthesized by hydrothermal method with L-aspartic acid and boric acid. Then the BN-CDs were anchored on the surface of MWCNTs by hydrothermal method and pyrolysis.

Fig. 1 a shows a typical X-ray diffraction (XRD) image of BN-CDs, with a broad peak at 24.1°; B1N0.5-CDs and B1N1.5-CDs have broad peaks at 26.5° and 23.9°, respectively (Fig. S1-2 , Supporting Information). The fluorescence spectra show excitation and emission wavelengths of BN-CDs as 355 nm and 430 nm, respectively (Fig. 1b), and strong blue photoluminescence (PL) was observed under ultraviolet (UV) (Fig. 1b, inset). The co-variation of excitation wavelength and emission wavelength indicates the BN-CDs exhibit excitation-dependence (Figure S3, Supporting Information). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) observed the morphology of the CDs: Fig. 1c shows well-dispersed CDs with an average particle size of 4.8 nm (Figure S4). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) determined the surface elemental composition and chemical state of the CDs (Figure S5), with the main elements being C (61.27 %), N (7.49 %), O (30.2 %), and B (1.04 %). The C 1s spectrum (Fig. 1d) indicates six different types of C: OC–O, CO, CN, CC, CC, and C–B. The N 1s spectrum (Fig. 1e) was fitted with four different peaks at 401.7, 401.1, 399.6, and 398.9 eV, which may be assigned to N4, graphitic N, pyrrolic N, and pyridinic N, respectively. The high-resolution B 1s spectrum (Fig. 1f) shows B–O (192.7 eV), BCO2 (192.3 eV), BC2O (191.8 eV), and BC3 (191 eV) peaks.

Various characterization methods further investigated the microstructure of the BN-CDs@CNT catalyst. Fig. 2a shows no obvious difference in the XRD patterns of the pure CNTs, BN-CDs@CNT, B-CNT, and N-CNT, indicating that their inherent properties did not change after heteroatom doping. The peaks at 26°, 42.8°, and 43.3° correspond to the (002), (100), and (101) crystal planes of graphene [27]. The peaks at 1339, 1569, and 1603 cm−1 in the Raman spectra (Fig. 2b) were assigned to the D, G and D′ bands. The appearance of the D and D′ bands indicates the existence of an intrinsic defect in the graphitic carbon. The D band is related to sp 3 carbon, representing defects of graphene; the G band is related to sp 2 carbon, representing the crystallinity of graphene. Therefore, the intensity ratio, ID/IG, represents a measure of the crystallinity and defects of carbon materials [28,29]. The ID/IG ratios for the four samples are ranked CNT > B-CNT > N-CNT > BN-CDs@CNT, possibly due to the addition of dopants to fill defects in MWCNTs during nitric acid treatment. The D′ band of BN-CDs@CNT is weaker than that of CNT, indicating a reduction in intrinsic defects. The morphology of BN-CDs@CNT was characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and TEM, which show (Figure S6) cracks on the surface of pure CNT after treatment, with no particles attached to the surface. However, Figure S7 indicates that BN-CDs are attached to the surface of MWCNTs, with the surface becoming smooth. The images is consistent with the results of the Raman spectrum. Ordered graphitization facilitates electron transfer, and the small size of the CDs is beneficial to reduction of the overpotential of the reaction [30,31]. The TEM image of the BN-CDs@CNT (Fig. 2c) clearly indicates BN-CDs anchored to the MWCNTs surface, with the attachment of small BN-CDs allowing a larger number of active sites to be exposed and MWCNTs providing a path for electron transfer. Furthermore, the BN-CDs have excellent edge active sites, which also provide many active sites for catalysis. New active sites were formed on the surfaces of MWCNTs during the heat treatment of the precursor of BN-CDs@CNT. The high-resolution Transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image (Fig. 2d) indicates MWCNTs interplanar spacings of 0.34 nm, less than the 0.39 nm corresponding to the graphene (002) crystal plane [32]. TEM and the Energy Dispersive X-Ray Spectroscopy (EDX) mapping images of BN-CDs@CNT confirm the presence of C, N, B, and O in the MWCNTs (Fig. 2e-i). Doping with N may change the electron distribution of adjacent C atoms, making them active sites, while doping with B is conducive to the adsorption of O and intermediates [33,34].

XPS further explored the surface chemical states and elemental compositions of BN-CDs@CNT (Fig. 3 a) with the main elements in CDs being C (96.36 %), N (1.19 %), O (1.95 %), and B (0.51 %), further confirming successful doping with B and N. The types of B and N are important in ORR, with different types playing different roles. XPS spectra for C 1s (Fig. 3b) can be fitted with five forms, CO, CO, CN, CC, and C–B, indicating B and N being successfully bonded to C. Four N species are indicated by peaks at 403, 401.1, 399.6, and 398.9 eV, corresponding to oxidized N, graphitic N, pyrrolic N, and pyridinic N, respectively (Fig. 3c). N has higher electronegativity than C, so the introduction of N atoms adjusts the electronic state of adjacent C atoms, facilitating chemisorption of O2. C atoms at the edge of armchair nanoribbons adjacent to N graphite dopant are considered the most active sites for ORR. The electron lone pair of pyridine N can actively induce charge transfer from the π orbital to the O2 anti-bonding orbital, weakening the OO bond and causing dissociation of OOH* intermediates to O* and OH*. Graphite N therefore enhances the electrocatalytic activity of C, while pyridine N contributes to electron transfer and the reduction of overpotential for ORR [35–37]. Qiao et al. found that the pyrrole N content is positively correlated with the yield of H2O2 [38]. The high-resolution B 1s XPS results for BN-CDs@CNT are shown in Fig. 3d, with the fitting of four peaks at 192.7, 192.3, 191.8, and 191 eV corresponding to B–O, CBO2, BC2O, and BC3, respectively. Although BC2O and BCO2 reduced the conductivity of BN-CDs@CNT due to the presence of BC2O and BCO2, the B atom can be appropriately combined with O intermediates such as OH or H2O to promote adsorption on the catalyst. In addition, the B atom replaces the sp 3 C atom to form the BC3 structure, redistributing the charge and forming polarized active catalytic sites [26]. More importantly, the synergy of pyridine N and BC3 markedly improves ORR performance, consistent with previous reports [14,39]. As Fig. 3e shows, the N2 adsorption–desorption isotherm (Fig. 3e) confirms that BN-CDs@CNT has a high specific surface area with many mesopores and micropores. This high specific area and numerous pores are conducive to diffusion of reaction species and electron transfer during electrocatalysis. However, the specific surface area of BN-CDs@CNT (136.2 m2 g−1) is smaller than that of pure MWCNTs (168 m2 g−1), possibly due to cracks on the surface of the pure MWCNTs being repaired by CDs, consistent with the SEM and Raman images.

ORR performance was evaluated for three-electrode systems with 0.1 M KOH saturated O2 solution and with 0.46 mg cm−2 catalyst supported on a rotating disk electrode (RDE). To obtain the optimal catalyst, different BN-CDs@CNT samples were prepared by changing experimental conditions, including the mass of CDs and calcination temperature and time, and their catalytic performances evaluated (Figure S8-12). BN-CDs@CNT synthesized with 400 mg of BN-CDs and 150 mg MWCNTs gave the best catalytic performance (Figure S8-10), with the optimal calcination temperature/time being 800 °C for 4 h (Figure S11-12). Optimal catalyst preparation conditions thus include 400 mg BN-CDs, and calcination at 800 °C for 4 h. ORR polarization curves and corresponding onset (Eonset) and half-wave (E1/2) potentials are shown in Fig. 4 a and b, respectively, with the latter indicating that BN-CDs@CNT has an excellent Eonset of 0.92 V vs reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) and a high E1/2 of 0.8 V vs RHE. These values are similar those of Pt/C (1.01 V and 0.85 V, respectively) and higher than those of the single-N-doped N-CNT catalyst (Eonset, 0.85 V; E1/2, 0.71 V) and the single-B-doped B-CNT catalyst (Eonset, 0.8 V; E1/2, 0.62 V). The highest content of pyridine N occurs in BN-CDs@CNT-4 h (Table S1), which may be the reason for the remarkably improved electrocatalytic performance. The electron-transfer number (n) of BN-CDs@CNT and the yield of H2O2 were obtained from the rotating ring-disk electrode (RRDE) test (Fig. 4c), with n averaging 3.48 and the H2O2 production rate being < 30 % in the voltage range 0.2–0.8 V. This indicates that both two- and four-electron reactions occurred, related to the content of pyrrole N in the catalyst. To further investigate catalytic kinetics, the Tafel slope was calculated from the ORR polarization curves. The Tafel slope of BN-CDs@CNT (72 mV Dec–1) is very similar to that of Pt/C (68 mV Dec–1) (Fig. 4d). The current density of the BN-CDs@CNT catalyst changed little after adding 1 M methanol (Fig. 4e), whereas that of Pt/C decreased rapidly. The resistance of BN-CDs@CNT to methanol makes it a potential candidate for fuel cells. Chronoamperometry measurements were performed for 10 h at a constant potential of 0.7 V in 0.1 M KOH solution to assess the stability of BN-CDs@CNT (Fig. 4f). The current density of BN-CDs@CNT was 76 % after a 10 h test while that of Pt/C dropped to 52 %, indicating good cycle stability. These results indicate that the BN-CDs@CNT catalyst provides outstanding ORR performance, which is superior to that of the most reported metal-free catalysts co-doped with boron and nitrogen (Table S2).

In summary, the BN-CDs@CNT catalyst was synthesized by hydrothermal method and pyrolysis with boron–nitrogen co-doped CDs without B-N bond and MWCNTs as raw materials. The as-prepared BN-CDs@CNT catalyst exhibits the low heteroatom doping and the excellent ORR performance. The remarkable improved ORR performance is attributed to the superiority of the structure of CDs and MWCNTs. Moreover, the synergistic effect of pyridine N and BC3 plays an important role in the catalytic process. This synthesis strategy of preparing double doped metal free catalysts by CDs engineering provides a new synthesis idea for metal-free catalysts.

Yanfei Pei: Investigation, Formal analysis, Visualization, Writing - original draft. Haoqiang Song: Visualization, Formal analysis, Writing - review & editing. Yuan Liu: Formal analysis, Writing - review & editing. Yaojia Cheng: Formal analysis, Writing - review & editing. Weidong Li: Formal analysis, Writing - review & editing. Yumei Chen: Supervision, Formal analysis, Visualization, Writing - review & editing. Yanping Fan: Formal analysis, Writing - review & editing. Baozhong Liu: Supervision, Formal analysis, Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition. Siyu Lu: Supervision, Formal analysis, Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (U1804135, 52071135, 51871090, 51671080, 21905253 and 51973200), Plan for Scientific Innovation Talent of Henan Province (194200510019) and the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2018M640681, 2019T120632)

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2021.05.089.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

Sci. 586 (2021) 621-629.

J. Colloid Interf. Sci.
